Example 4 (Rotation of a rigid body) I The rotation of a rigid body in space can be described by a vector ω ⃗ , called the angular velocity. ω ⃗ I The direction of ω⃗ coincides with the axis of rotation, and ⃗v the rotation appears clockwise if one looks from the initial P b d point of ω ⃗ to its terminal point, (or counterclockwise if one looks from the terminal point to initial point of ω⃗ ). ω ⃗ I The magnitude of ω ⃗ is the angular speed ω. ⃗r ⃗r ⃗0 ⃗0 I If the axis of rotation passes through the origin O, then ⃗ × ⃗r. the linear velocity at P is ⃗v = ω

Kinematics I In general, we can use a time-dependent position vector Vectors can be used to describe the motion of a particle ⃗r(t) to represent any parametric path through space: travelling through space. We have already seen a simple example of this with the parametric equation for a straight line: ⃗r(t) = x(t)⃗i + y(t) ⃗j + z(t) ⃗k.       x(t) x0 b1 I The velocity of the particle is the time derivative of the ⃗r(t) := y(t) =  y0  + t b2  = ⃗r0 + t⃗b. position: z(t) z0 b3 d⃗r dx ⃗ dy ⃗ dz ⃗ ⃗v (t) = = i+ j+ k, Here, ⃗r(t) = x(t)⃗i + y(t) ⃗j + z(t) ⃗k is the position of the dt dt dt dt particle at time t, and in particular and the acceleration is the time derivative of the velocity, ⃗r(0) = ⃗r0 = x0 ⃗i + y0 ⃗j + z0 ⃗k d⃗v d2⃗r d2 x d2 y d2 z ⃗a(t) = = 2 = 2 ⃗i + 2 ⃗j + 2 ⃗k. is the initial position at time t = 0. Also, ⃗b = b1 ⃗i + b2 ⃗j + b3 ⃗k dt dt dt dt dt is the (constant) velocity vector, which points in the direction of motion. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . (Thus, the velocity is a vector but The vector ∆⃗r(t) points along the path travelled by the the speed is a scalar.) particle and will become tangential to the path . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . as ∆t → 0. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Example 7 Consider the path in R2 given by ⃗r(t) = cos(t)⃗i + sin(t) ⃗j. 1. Show that the path is a circle. 2. Prove that the velocity vector is always tangential to the circle. 3. Prove that the acceleration of the particle is directed to the centre of the circle. (This is always the case for uniform circular motion.)

Vector fields

→ − I A 2D vector field is a function f⃗ : R2 → R2 , that is, a I Another example of a vector field is the gradient ∇ϕ of a function that assigns a 2D vector f⃗(x, y) to every scalar field ϕ, which tells us the direction and magnitude point (x, y) in the plane. of maximum increase of the scalar field ϕ. It is important to remember that we think of each vector in a vector field I Similarly, a 3D vector field f⃗ : R3 → R3 assigns a 3D as assigned to a particular point in space and not with its vector f⃗(x, y, z) to each point (x, y, z) in space. base at the origin. I An example of a 3D vector field is the air velocity in a I Vector fields provide the natural mathematical tool for room. We could imagine measuring the air velocity (speed studying all kinds of fluid (gas or liquid) flows. and direction) at every point in space. There would be large vectors near the AC ducts and small vectors in the corners of the room where there is little air movement.

Example 13 (Inverse square law of gravitation) Gradient, Divergence, and Curl Consider a particle of mass M located at a point P0 , and a second particle of mass m located at a point P . According to Newton, the gravitational force F⃗G on m at P due to M at P0 has magnitude Earlier, we introduced the vector differentiation operator, del GM m or nabla, ∥F⃗G ∥ = , → − ∂ ⃗ ∂ ⃗ ∂ ⃗ r2 ∇= i+ j+ k, ∂x ∂y ∂z where G ≈ 6.67430 × 10−11 m3 kg−1 s−2 is the gravitational constant and r is the distance between P and P0 . and defined the gradient of a scalar field ϕ to be the vector The gravitational force F⃗G is a vector field, given by field − → ∂ϕ ⃗ ∂ϕ ⃗ ∂ϕ ⃗ grad ϕ = ∇ϕ = i+ j+ k. ⃗r GM m ∂x ∂y ∂z F⃗G = −∥F⃗G ∥ = − 3 ⃗r, r r −−→ where ⃗r = P0 P . The minus sign indicates that F⃗G is directed from P to P0 , that is, gravity is an attractive force. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .   Notes Definition 14 I Each of grad ϕ, curl F⃗ and div F⃗ is a Euclidean invariant. Given a vector field F⃗ (x, y, z) = F1 ⃗i + F2 ⃗j + F3 ⃗k, the curl of F⃗ is the vector field defined by I The operators grad, div, and curl are linear:

⃗i ⃗j ⃗k grad(aϕ + bψ) = a grad ϕ + b grad ψ, → − curl F⃗ = ∇ × F⃗ = ∂ ∂x ∂ ∂y ∂ ∂z , curl(aF⃗ + bG) ⃗ = a curl F⃗ + b curl G, ⃗ F1 F2 F3 div(aF⃗ + bG) ⃗ = a div F⃗ + b div G. ⃗

and the divergence of F⃗ is the scalar field defined by I Expanding the determinant, we find that → − ∂F1 ∂F2 ∂F3 div F⃗ = ∇ · F⃗ = + + . curl F⃗ = ∂x ∂y ∂z       ∂F3 ∂F2 ⃗ ∂F1 ∂F3 ⃗ ∂F2 ∂F1 ⃗ − i+ − j+ − k. ∂y ∂z ∂z ∂x ∂x ∂y

Physical interpretation of div F⃗

I The divergence of a vector field tells us the rate at which the flow represented by the vector field diverges (positive divergence) or converges (negative divergence) at a particular point. I The divergence also reflects the presence of sources (where divergence is positive) or sinks (where divergence is negative) in the flow. Examples of divergent and convergent vector fields are shown in Figure 4. The flow will diverge away from a source and converge towards a Figure: (Left) Positive divergence. (Right) Negative divergence sink. (convergence).

. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .  I Assume that the flow is steady, that is, independent of I Q: What would happen if the water is moving at a time. constant speed in one direction, for example in a strongly I The product of the fluid density ρ (a scalar) and the fluid flowing river? velocity ⃗v (a vector) is a vector field J⃗ = ρ⃗v called the I A: R = 0 because all the water entering the cube leaves mass flux density. at the same rate on the other side. There is no net I Imagine placing a small wire cube in the fluid so that outflow of water from the cube. water can flow through any of its six faces. We want to measure R, the rate of outflow divided by the volume of I Now imagine that we place a strong source of water the cube, in the limit as the side length of the cube tends inside the cube, for example a hose. Then R will be to zero. positive because water leaves the cube on all of its faces. Alternatively, if we placed a sink of water inside the cube, for example a drain hole, then R will be negative because water is entering the cube on all of its faces. b

I The rate of outflow R ∆x ∆y ∆z must equal the rate at which the enclosed mass decreases (R > 0) or increases (R < 0). . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

∆y J1 (x, y, z) I The rate of outflow through the right-hand face of the ∆z cube is (approximately) J1 (x0 + 12 ∆x, y0 , z0 ) ∆y ∆z. ∆x I There is no flow through the other four sides of the cube I Assume first that the flow is in the x-direction so that because they are parallel to the direction of the fluid J⃗ = J1 ⃗i. Let (x0 , y0 , z0 ) be the coordinates of the centre velocity. of the cube. The rate at which mass enters through the left-hand face of the cube is (approximately) I So the total rate of outflow is J1 (x0 − 12 ∆x, y0 , z0 ) ∆y ∆z, so the rate of outflow through this face is −J1 (x0 − 12 ∆x, y0 , z0 ) ∆y ∆z. Notice R(x0 , y0 , z0 ) ∆x ∆y ∆z ≈ J1 (x0 + 12 ∆x, y0 , z0 ) ∆y ∆z that if J1 > 0 then the outflow is negative because fluid − J1 (x0 − 12 ∆x, y0 , z0 ) ∆y ∆z. is flowing into the cube on the left side. I In general, we multiply the outward normal component of the flux density vector by the area of the face to get the outward flux through that face. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .  I After dividing by the volume ∆x ∆y ∆z of the cube and taking the limit as the side lengths tend to zero, we Example 18 obtain the rate of outflow per unit volume, Consider the vector field F⃗ (x, y, z) = x2 ⃗i + y 2 ⃗j. 1. Calculate the divergence of F⃗ . R(x0 , y0 , z0 ) 2. Show in the xy plane where the vector field has positive, J1 (x0 + 12 ∆x, y0 , z0 ) − J1 (x0 − 12 ∆x, y0 , z0 ) negative, and zero divergence. = lim ∆x→0 ∆x ∂J1 = (x0 , y0 , z0 ). ∂x

I We can repeat this process in each direction for a vector field F⃗ = J1 ⃗i + J2 ⃗j + J3 ⃗k. Then the net outflow of water on all six sides is ∂J1 ∂J2 ∂J3 − → ⃗ R= + + = ∇ · J. ∂x ∂y ∂z → ⃗ − ∇ · F = 2x + 2y. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Conservation of Mass

I Now permit unsteady flow with density ρ(⃗r, t) and velocity ⃗v (⃗r, t).

I Mass flux density J⃗ = ρ⃗v satisfies − → ⃗ ( ∇ · J) ∆x ∆y ∆z = net outflow from cube = rate at which mass leaves cube.

I Total mass enclosed by cube is ρ ∆x ∆y ∆z.

Figure: F⃗ (x, y, z) = x2 ⃗i + y 2 ⃗j (Example 18) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .   I If mass is conserved (no sources or sinks present) then I Let f (⃗r, t) be some material property of the fluid, such as ∂ temperature or density. Circulation around a tiny square I Consider a tiny square parallel to the xy-plane with sides I Hence, ∆x and ∆y, in the presence of a flow with velocity field ⃗i ⃗j ⃗k ⃗v (x, y, z) = v1 (x, y, z)⃗i + v2 (x, y, z) ⃗j + v3 (x, y, z) ⃗k. → − ∇ × ⃗v = ω ∂/∂x ∂/∂y ∂/∂z I For each side, we multiply the component of ⃗v in the −y x 0 forward tangential direction by the length of the side, = ω(0⃗i − 0 ⃗j + 2 ⃗k) = 2ω ⃗k where “forward” means counterclockwise looking down along the z axis. − → I Therefore, ∥ ∇ × ⃗v ∥ = 2ω = twice the angular speed, as I Summing these quantities gives the circulation of ⃗v claimed earlier. around the square. I A positive circulation means the flow tends to rotate counterclockwise (looking down along the z axis).
